selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: You know, I don't have social media myself, and honestly, I think that's the best decision I ever made. At eighty-seven years old, I've seen enough in this world to know when something's more trouble than it's worth. People ask me all the time at the restaurant where I work part-time how to deal with all the fake news and false information online, and I tell them straight up: the easiest way to manage fake content is to not be on those platforms in the first place. I know that sounds simplistic, but hear me out. When I talk to customers during my shifts, so many of them are anxious, worried, constantly checking their phones, getting worked up over things they read that turn out to be completely fabricated. It's exhausting just watching them. I've lived through enough real crises without needing manufactured ones delivered to my pocket every five minutes. The way I see it, if something's truly important, you'll hear about it through traditional news sources or from actual people in your life. When I'm volunteering or at the cinema or discussing the latest calcio match with friends, we talk face to face. There's accountability there. You can see someone's eyes, judge their character, ask questions directly. That's how humans communicated for thousands of years, and it worked fine. Now I'm not saying I'm perfect about staying informed. I read novels, I catch the news on television, I listen to what people tell me. But I filter everything through lived experience and common sense. If something sounds too outrageous or too perfectly aligned with making people angry, I'm skeptical. That's just wisdom that comes with age. For people who insist on using social media, I'd say trust your gut more and your screen less. Talk to real people. Get multiple perspectives. And remember that companies running these platforms profit from your engagement, not from your wellbeing. They don't care if what you're reading is true. At my age, dealing with depression and having lost my spouse, I've learned that protecting your mental space matters more than staying connected to every trending controversy. Sometimes disconnection is the healthiest choice you can make.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 87-year-old individual with no social media presence, which is the most defining characteristic for this topic. Their high extraversion suggests they value real human connection and conversation, while their high neuroticism and depression indicate sensitivity to negative stimuli that social media could exacerbate. Their low openness suggests preference for traditional methods and skepticism toward new technologies. Working part-time as a waiter provides daily interaction with people who likely discuss social media issues. Their hobbies are all offline activities, reinforcing a lifestyle without digital social platforms. The tone should be direct, somewhat world-weary but conversational given the high extraversion, with touches of concern and skepticism. The perspective naturally flows from someone who has opted out entirely and sees this as vindication rather than limitation.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, conversational, world-weary, skeptical, concerned but practical
